myrcene.name: Myrcene
myrcene.description: The most abundant terpene in cannabis. Myrcene is commonly associated with the sedating, couch-lock experience often attributed to indica strains. It is thought to influence how cannabinoids interact with the body.
myrcene.medicalResearch: Preliminary studies are exploring potential properties. Some research suggests it may influence cell membrane permeability. More human clinical trials are needed.

limonene.name: Limonene
limonene.description: A bright citrus-scented terpene commonly associated with mood elevation and energizing qualities. Limonene is a hallmark of many sativa-dominant strains and is also widely used in food and cosmetic products.
limonene.medicalResearch: Some research is exploring its potential mood-related and calming properties. It is widely used in food and cosmetics with a long safety record. Clinical studies in humans are ongoing.

linalool.name: Linalool
linalool.description: Best known for its presence in lavender, linalool is commonly associated with calming and relaxing experiences. It is one of the most widely used terpenes in aromatherapy and may temper the anxious edge that THC can produce in some people.
linalool.medicalResearch: Some studies have explored its calming properties, particularly in aromatherapy contexts. Research is ongoing to understand its interactions with other compounds. Not a substitute for medical treatment.

caryophyllene.name: Caryophyllene
caryophyllene.description: The only terpene known to interact with CB2 cannabinoid receptors, making it unique among terpenes. Researchers are studying this interaction to better understand how it may influence the body without psychoactive effects.
caryophyllene.medicalResearch: Its unique CB2 receptor interaction is an active area of scientific research. Some preclinical studies have explored potential properties. Human clinical data is limited; more research is needed.

pinene.name: Pinene
pinene.description: The most common terpene found in nature, responsible for the fresh scent of pine forests. Pinene is commonly associated with alertness and mental clarity among cannabis users.
pinene.medicalResearch: Some preclinical research has explored its potential properties. It is widely found in nature with a long history of use in folk remedies. Human clinical studies are needed.

terpinolene.name: Terpinolene
terpinolene.description: A complex, multifaceted terpene found in small amounts in many strains. Terpinolene is often described as uplifting and creative at lower concentrations. Strains dominant in terpinolene are relatively rare and sought after.
terpinolene.medicalResearch: Some early preclinical studies have explored potential properties. Research is in early stages and human clinical data is limited.

humulene.name: Humulene
humulene.description: Sharing a close relationship with caryophyllene, humulene is notable for community reports of appetite-moderating properties. It provides subtle earthy and woody notes and contributes to the overall terpene profile.
humulene.medicalResearch: Some preclinical research has explored various properties. Unlike many cannabis-related compounds, it is sometimes associated with reduced appetite in anecdotal reports. More research is needed.

ocimene.name: Ocimene
ocimene.description: A delicate, sweet-scented terpene commonly associated with energizing experiences. Ocimene is found in many plants traditionally used in folk remedies and contributes to the aromatic complexity of cannabis.
ocimene.medicalResearch: Some preclinical research has explored various properties. It is commonly found in nature. Human clinical studies are needed to validate any specific effects.

bisabolol.name: Bisabolol
bisabolol.description: A gentle terpene best known as the primary component of chamomile essential oil. Bisabolol is widely used in skincare products. In cannabis, it adds a subtle sweetness and is commonly associated with calming experiences.
bisabolol.medicalResearch: Widely used in cosmetics with a long safety record. Some studies have explored various properties. As with all terpenes, human clinical trials specific to cannabis contexts are limited.
